Author: Mojtaba Hashemi
Title: Feasibility and Requirements in Software Engineering — A Practical View
Text:
Feasibility and Requirements in Software Engineering — A Practical View
Almost all of us have been on a project that started with great energy but got stuck somewhere along the way — or worse, delivered something that wasn’t what the client really wanted. Interestingly, in most of these failures, the issue wasn’t technology, the team, or even the schedule. The real problem started way earlier — before a single line of an SRS (Software Requirements Specification) was written — in a place we often overlook: feasibility and requirements analysis.
What Are Feasibility and Requirements?
In software engineering, before we dive into formal requirements analysis, the architect and analyst must take one step back and ask two simple questions:
- Is this project actually doable?
- And if it is, what specific need are we solving?
These questions are the essence of feasibility and requirements — but answering them isn’t easy. Requirements aren’t just a list of what the client says they want. Often, what the client describes as a solution is actually just their mental image of the fix — not the real problem. That’s where the analyst’s skill matters: asking the right questions, listening, and sometimes even challenging assumptions to uncover the true business need.
Feasibility Is About Reality, Not Paper
Feasibility is about grounding ideas in real-world facts:
- Do we have the budget and time?
- Does the team have the right skills?
- Is the technology stack capable of delivering the solution?
- Can infrastructure support it?
The software architect needs to see the big picture and decide if the idea is practical — or just pretty on paper. Often during feasibility analysis, some requirements must be simplified, redefined, or even removed. That’s not failure — it’s professional insight that saves time and money down the line.
Why Getting This Step Right Matters
Before writing the SRS, analysts and stakeholders should align not only on what to build but also why, for whom, and under which constraints. If this deeper understanding doesn’t happen, the SRS becomes nothing more than a nicely formatted but essentially meaningless document.
The Human Side of Engineering
In reality, feasibility and requirements are more human activities than documentation tasks. They involve conversations, listening, analysis, doubts, and decisions — things that can’t be fully captured by checklists or templates. This phase is where software engineering actually becomes engineering: a blend of logic, experience, and human insight.
